topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam before my eyes, a grotesque stain across the pristine white page. A C-minus. A C-minus on my final paper. I stared at the grade as if it were a venomous snake coiled around my throat. My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge to keep them steady. A sharp, hot pressure built behind my sternum, a frantic drumming that drowned out the ambient noise of the library. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. I snatched the paper, crumpled it into a tight, furious ball, and threw it toward the overflowing waste bin. The sound of the paper hitting the metal was shockingly loud in the quiet study room. My breath hitched, shallow and ragged, and I felt a sudden, dizzying urge to run, to flee the suffocating weight of that number.

[story 2]

I reread the feedback, the professor’s marginal notes seeming to mock me with their clinical detachment. It wasn't just a low mark; it felt like a personal indictment. My vision tunneled; the fluorescent lights above seemed to buzz with an unbearable frequency. I paced the small space between the bookshelf and the window, my steps quick and jerky, like a trapped animal. I stopped abruptly, pressing the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically push the numbers out of my sight. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a strangled gasp that caught in my chest. Every nerve ending felt exposed, buzzing with a high-pitched tension. I squeezed my fists until my knuckles were white, the sheer imbalance of the situation making my stomach clench with a painful, churning dread.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my screen, stark and unforgiving: 68%. I didn't move for a full minute, just sitting there, a statue carved from sheer disbelief. Then, the stillness shattered. I pushed my chair back so fast it scraped loudly against the linoleum floor. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that felt dangerously close to tearing something inside me apart. I started pacing the length of the room, my movements becoming erratic, sharp little bursts of energy. I stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent campus quad, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles protesting. A desperate need to shout, to scream the injustice of it all, built up behind my eyes, a pressure that made my head swim. I just stood there, breathing in sharp, shallow gulps of air, completely overwhelmed by the sheer wrongness of the outcome.
